title: Schema Validate
description: Schema authors and project maintainers need a way to verify that their schema configuration is correct before other commands consume it. Errors in schema YAML â€” malformed structure, invalid artifact IDs, dependency cycles, missing templates â€” currently surface only as side effects of unrelated commands. The `specd schema validate` command provides a dedicated validation entry point that delegates to the `ValidateSchema` use case in core, keeping the CLI as a thin adapter.
context: Schema authors and project maintainers need a way to verify that their schema configuration is correct before other commands consume it. Errors in schema YAML â€” malformed structure, invalid artifact IDs, dependency cycles, missing templates â€” currently surface only as side effects of unrelated commands. The `specd schema validate` command provides a dedicated validation entry point that delegates to the `ValidateSchema` use case in core, keeping the CLI as a thin adapter.

rules:
`specd schema validate [ref] [--file <path>] [--raw] [--format text|json|toon] [--config <path>]`

- `[ref]` â€” optional positional argument; a schema reference in the same format as `specd.yaml`'s `schema` field (e.g. `@specd/schema-std`, `#workspace:name`, `#name`, bare name). Resolved through `SchemaRegistry`.
- When neither `[ref]`, `--file`, nor `--raw` is provided, the command validates the project's fully-resolved schema (merged).
- When `--raw` is provided (without `[ref]` or `--file`), the command validates the project's base schema without plugins or overrides.
- When `--file` is provided, the command validates the specified external file (resolving its extends chain).
- When `[ref]` is provided, the command validates the referenced schema (resolving its extends chain, without project plugins or overrides).
- `[ref]`, `--file`, and `--raw` are mutually exclusive. If any combination is provided, the command SHALL fail with an error.

When invoked without `--file` or `--raw`, the command SHALL call `ValidateSchema.execute` with `mode: 'project'`, passing `schemaRef`, `schemaPlugins`, and `schemaOverrides` from the project config. This runs the full resolution pipeline.

When invoked with `--raw` (without `--file`), the command SHALL call `ValidateSchema.execute` with `mode: 'project-raw'`, passing only `schemaRef`. This validates the base schema with its extends chain but without plugins or overrides.

When invoked with `--file <path>`, the command SHALL call `ValidateSchema.execute` with `mode: 'file'` and the resolved absolute path. The use case handles extends resolution, file reading, and validation.

When invoked with `[ref]` (without `--file` or `--raw`), the command SHALL call `ValidateSchema.execute` with `mode: 'ref'` and the provided reference string. The use case handles resolution through `SchemaRegistry`, extends chain resolution, and validation. No project plugins or overrides are applied.

When validation succeeds and `--format` is `text`, the command SHALL write:

- Project resolved mode: `schema valid: <name> v<version> (<N> artifacts, <M> workflow steps)`
- Project raw mode: `schema valid: <name> v<version> (<N> artifacts, <M> workflow steps) [raw]`
- File mode: `schema valid: <name> v<version> (<N> artifacts, <M> workflow steps) [file]`
- Ref mode: `schema valid: <name> v<version> (<N> artifacts, <M> workflow steps) [ref]`

Warning lines, if any, are appended as `  warning: <text>`.
When validation fails and `--format` is `text`, the command SHALL write `schema validation failed:` followed by indented error lines, one per validation error.

When `--format` is `json` or `toon`, the command SHALL write a structured result object:

Success:

```json
{
  "result": "ok",
  "schema": { "name": "...", "version": 1 },
  "artifacts": 5,
  "workflowSteps": 4,
  "mode": "project" | "project-raw" | "file" | "ref",
  "warnings": []
}
```

Failure:

```json
{
  "result": "error",
  "errors": [{ "message": "..." }],
  "warnings": [],
  "mode": "project" | "project-raw" | "file" | "ref"
}
```
The command SHALL exit with code 0 when the schema is valid and code 1 when validation fails.
When `--file <path>` references a file that does not exist, the validation result SHALL contain the error and exit with code 1.
When `[ref]` references a schema that cannot be resolved (e.g. npm package not installed, workspace schema does not exist), the validation result SHALL contain the error and exit with code 1.

rules.requirement: Error â€” config required in project modes
rules: When invoked without `--file` and no `specd.yaml` can be discovered, the command SHALL fail with the standard config-not-found error (as defined by the entrypoint spec).

`[ref]`, `--file`, and `--raw` are mutually exclusive. If any combination of two or more is provided, the command SHALL write a descriptive error and exit with code 1:

- `[ref]` + `--file`: `[ref] and --file are mutually exclusive`
- `[ref]` + `--raw`: `[ref] and --raw are mutually exclusive`
- `--file` + `--raw`: `--file and --raw are mutually exclusive`

constraints: The command contains no validation logic â€” all validation is performed by the ValidateSchema use case in core
The command follows all global CLI conventions (exit codes, --format, --config, excess argument rejection)
The CLI imports only use-case factories or the kernel â€” never domain services or infrastructure functions directly

scenarios.name: No flags defaults to project resolved mode
when: the user runs specd schema validate without [ref], --file, or --raw
then: the command validates the project's fully-resolved schema

scenarios.name: --raw triggers project raw mode
when: the user runs specd schema validate --raw
then: the command validates only the base schema without plugins or overrides

scenarios.name: --file triggers file mode
when: the user runs specd schema validate --file ./my-schema.yaml
then: the command validates the specified file with extends resolution

scenarios.name: [ref] triggers ref mode
when: the user runs specd schema validate @specd/schema-std
then: the command validates the referenced schema with extends resolution

scenarios.name: Valid resolved project schema
given: a project with a valid specd.yaml and a valid schema
when: the user runs specd schema validate
then: AND output confirms the schema is valid

scenarios.name: Resolved schema with invalid plugin
given: a project whose config references a plugin that does not exist
when: the user runs specd schema validate
then: AND the error identifies the missing plugin

scenarios.name: Valid raw project schema
given: a project with a valid base schema
when: the user runs specd schema validate --raw

scenarios.name: Raw mode isolates base errors from plugin errors
given: a project whose base schema is valid but a plugin introduces an error
when: the user runs specd schema validate --raw
then: exit code is 0 (base is valid regardless of plugin issues)

scenarios.name: Valid external file
given: a syntactically and semantically valid schema.yaml file
when: the user runs specd schema validate --file ./schema.yaml

scenarios.name: External file with extends resolves chain
given: a schema file that declares extends: @specd/schema-std
when: the user runs specd schema validate --file ./schema.yaml

scenarios.name: External file with invalid artifact ID
given: a schema.yaml with an artifact whose id is Invalid_ID
when: the user runs specd schema validate --file ./schema.yaml
then: AND the error mentions the invalid artifact ID

scenarios.name: External file with circular extends
given: a schema file whose extends chain forms a cycle
when: the user runs specd schema validate --file ./schema.yaml
then: AND the error mentions cycle detection

scenarios.name: Valid schema resolved by ref
given: @specd/schema-std is installed as an npm package
when: the user runs specd schema validate @specd/schema-std

scenarios.name: Ref with extends resolves chain
given: a workspace schema #default:my-schema that declares extends: @specd/schema-std
when: the user runs specd schema validate '#default:my-schema'

scenarios.name: Ref with invalid schema content
given: a workspace schema with an invalid artifact ID
when: the user runs specd schema validate '#default:bad-schema'
then: AND the error mentions the invalid artifact ID

scenarios.name: Project resolved mode success text
when: project resolved validation succeeds with schema my-schema v1 with 3 artifacts and 2 workflow steps
then: stdout contains schema valid: my-schema v1 (3 artifacts, 2 workflow steps)

scenarios.name: Project raw mode success text
when: project raw validation succeeds

scenarios.name: File mode success text
when: file mode validation succeeds

scenarios.name: Ref mode success text
when: ref mode validation succeeds

scenarios.name: Failure text lists errors
when: validation fails with two errors
then: stdout contains schema validation failed: followed by two indented error lines

scenarios.name: JSON success contains expected keys
when: the user runs specd schema validate --format json and validation succeeds
then: the JSON output contains result, schema, artifacts, workflowSteps, mode, and warnings

scenarios.name: JSON failure contains error details
when: the user runs specd schema validate --format json and validation fails
then: the JSON output contains result: "error", errors, warnings, and mode

scenarios.name: JSON ref mode includes mode ref
when: the user runs specd schema validate @specd/schema-std --format json and validation succeeds

scenarios.name: Exit 0 on valid schema
when: validation succeeds

scenarios.name: Exit 1 on invalid schema
when: validation fails

scenarios.name: --file with nonexistent path
when: the user runs specd schema validate --file ./nonexistent.yaml

scenarios.name: [ref] with nonexistent schema
when: the user runs specd schema validate @nonexistent/schema

scenarios.requirement: Error â€” config required in project modes
scenarios.name: No specd.yaml discoverable
given: CWD is not under any directory containing specd.yaml
when: the user runs specd schema validate without --file and without --config
then: the command fails with the standard config-not-found error

scenarios.name: --file and --raw together
when: the user runs specd schema validate --file ./schema.yaml --raw
then: stderr contains --file and --raw are mutually exclusive

scenarios.name: [ref] and --file together
when: the user runs specd schema validate @specd/schema-std --file ./schema.yaml
then: stderr contains [ref] and --file are mutually exclusive

scenarios.name: [ref] and --raw together
when: the user runs specd schema validate @specd/schema-std --raw
then: stderr contains [ref] and --raw are mutually exclusive
